content.text: Open-weight models are large language models whose trained parameter checkpoints are publicly downloadable. You run them on your own hardware — a laptop with a capable GPU, a cloud VM, or a cluster — with no API dependency, no per-token cost, and no data leaving your infrastructure. The leading families are Meta's LLaMA 3, Mistral, Google's Gemma, and Alibaba's Qwen. "Open-weight" is the precise term: the weights are released, but the training code and data often are not, and licences vary significantly. Practical open-weight work involves choosing the right model size, quantising to fit available memory, and serving efficiently.
Loading and Running a Model Locally
Hugging Face Transformers is the standard library. A model is downloaded once and cached; subsequent runs load from disk:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch


def load_open_weight_model(model_id: str):
    """
    Download (first run) and load an open-weight model.
    Weights are cached in ~/.cache/huggingface/hub/.

    model_id examples:
      "mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3"   (7B, Apache 2.0)
      "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"  (8B, Meta Community)
      "google/gemma-2-9b-it"                  (9B, Gemma ToU)
      "Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct"             (7B, Apache 2.0)
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_id,
        torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,  # halves memory vs float32; BF16 is stable
        device_map="auto",            # shards across GPUs; offloads to CPU if needed
    )
    model.eval()
    return model, tokenizer


def generate(model, tokenizer, messages: list[dict], max_new_tokens: int = 256) -> str:
    input_ids = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        messages,
        tokenize=True,
        add_generation_prompt=True,
        return_tensors="pt",
    ).to(model.device)

    with torch.no_grad():
        output_ids = model.generate(
            input_ids,
            max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
            do_sample=True,
            temperature=0.7,
            top_p=0.9,
            pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
        )
    new_ids = output_ids[0, input_ids.shape[1]:]
    return tokenizer.decode(new_ids, skip_special_tokens=True)


# model, tok = load_open_weight_model("mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3")
# print(generate(model, tok, [{"role": "user", "content": "What is entropy?"}]))
Quantisation: Fitting Models into Available Memory

A 7B model in float16 occupies ~14 GB of VRAM — more than most consumer GPUs have. Quantisation compresses weights to 4- or 8-bit integers, reducing memory by 2–4× with minimal quality loss. The most practical options are GPTQ (post-training quantisation, static) and BitsAndBytes (on-the-fly, easy to use):

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch


def load_4bit_quantised(model_id: str):
    """
    Load an open-weight model in 4-bit NF4 quantisation using bitsandbytes.
    A 7B model drops from ~14 GB to ~4.5 GB VRAM — fits on a single RTX 3080.
    """
    bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
        load_in_4bit=True,
        bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",        # NormalFloat4: better than int4 for LLMs
        bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,   # quantise the quantisation constants too
        bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    )
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_id,
        quantization_config=bnb_config,
        device_map="auto",
    )
    return model, tokenizer


# Memory comparison (approximate, 7B model):
# float32        → 28 GB
# bfloat16       → 14 GB
# 8-bit int      →  7 GB
# 4-bit NF4      →  4.5 GB   ← fits on RTX 3080 (10 GB) or 3090 (24 GB)
Efficient Serving with llama.cpp and Ollama

For inference-only use, llama.cpp (via the GGUF format) and Ollama are more efficient than full PyTorch: they use CPU SIMD and partial GPU offload, handle quantisation natively, and expose an OpenAI-compatible API on localhost:

# Ollama: run `ollama pull mistral` once, then serve on http://localhost:11434
# The API is OpenAI-compatible, so the same client works.

from openai import OpenAI

# Point the OpenAI client at your local Ollama instance.
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",
    api_key="ollama",    # required by the client but not checked by Ollama
)


def local_chat(user_message: str, model: str = "mistral") -> str:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content


print(local_chat("Summarise the transformer architecture in three bullet points."))
# Runs entirely on local hardware; no network call to any external API.
Best overall quality; 128k context; huge ecosystem
Strong at its size; 2B is edge-deployable
Multilingual; strong code/math; broad size range
Exceptional quality per parameter; runs on CPU
Medical records, legal documents, or proprietary code cannot be sent to a third-party API. Running locally keeps data within your perimeter.
At millions of tokens per day, self-hosting amortises GPU costs below API prices. The break-even is typically several hundred dollars per month of API spend.
You cannot fine-tune most API models on custom data. Open weights let you run LoRA or full fine-tuning on your own dataset.
A model running on local hardware has sub-100ms time-to-first-token with no network round-trip and continues working without internet access.

"Open-weight" does not mean free in all senses: licences differ, some impose commercial restrictions, and the infrastructure cost is real. But open-weight models give you something API models cannot: full control. You can inspect the weights, modify them, quantise them, fine-tune them, and run them air-gapped. For many production use cases that control is not needed; for some it is non-negotiable.

For the first several years of the LLM era, using a capable language model meant sending your data to an API operated by a third party. That model created an irreducible dependency: the vendor chose the model, the vendor could change or discontinue it, the vendor could observe your prompts, and the vendor charged per token. Open-weight models break every one of these dependencies. You download the weights once, run them on hardware you control, and have full authority over the model's behaviour, availability, and cost structure.

Open-weight models have reached near-parity with closed models for many tasks. LLaMA 3 70B matches GPT-3.5 on most benchmarks; Qwen 2.5 72B competes with GPT-4o on coding tasks. This quality gap narrowed sharply between 2023 and 2025, driven by distillation from stronger teacher models, synthetic data generated by closed models, and increasingly sophisticated post-training alignment. Practitioners who dismissed open-weight models as 'research-grade' tools in 2023 should re-evaluate that assumption against current releases.

The choice between open-weight and closed models is increasingly a business and compliance decision rather than a capability decision. GDPR and HIPAA impose obligations on data processors — sending patient records or personal data to a third-party API may require a Data Processing Agreement and creates audit liability. An open-weight model running on your own infrastructure is not a data processor in the regulatory sense; it is software you operate. For legal, medical, and financial applications, this distinction is non-negotiable.

An open-weight model is a neural network whose trained parameter checkpoint is publicly downloadable, typically from Hugging Face Hub or the model creator's own server. The weights are the model — the billions of floating-point numbers that encode all the learned knowledge. With the weights, anyone who has compatible hardware and the inference code can run the model without network connectivity, without API keys, and without per-query payments.

The term 'open-weight' is more precise than 'open-source' for most models in this category. True open-source would include the training code, the training data, and a permissive licence. Most open-weight releases provide the weights and an inference licence but omit the training data and sometimes the training code. LLaMA 3's Community Licence, for example, restricts redistribution and commercial use above certain scales. Apache 2.0 models (Mistral, Qwen) are the most permissively licensed. Treat licence compatibility as a first-class engineering constraint.

Running an open-weight model is not free — it transfers cost from per-token API fees to infrastructure (GPU hardware or cloud VM rental), engineering time (deployment, monitoring, updates), and operational risk (the model is your responsibility). For low-volume use cases, the API is almost always cheaper. Open-weight deployment becomes cost-effective only when token volume is high or when data privacy/regulatory requirements make APIs unavailable.
A 7B model in float16 needs ~14 GB VRAM. A 70B model needs ~140 GB VRAM — two A100 80GB GPUs. Consumer hardware can run smaller models with quantisation but not the largest ones.
API providers push safety patches and capability improvements automatically. With open-weight models you must track upstream releases, evaluate new versions, and redeploy manually.
Closed APIs provide content filtering. Open-weight models ship with alignment (RLHF/DPO) but you can remove it. You are responsible for output safety in your deployment.
A single GPU running a 7B model generates ~40 tokens/second. API providers scale horizontally to thousands of GPU-hours. For burst traffic, the API is hard to beat.
How it works — loading and inference internals

A model checkpoint is a collection of tensor files (typically safetensors format for safety, or PyTorch .bin files). The architecture is defined in a config.json that specifies layer count, hidden size, attention heads, and context length. The tokeniser is defined separately (tokenizer.json + tokenizer_config.json). The Hugging Face transformers library reads these files and reconstructs the full model graph in memory.

At inference time, tokens are processed left-to-right through the transformer stack. Each forward pass through the model produces logits over the entire vocabulary (typically 32,000–128,000 tokens). A sampling strategy (greedy, top-k, top-p, temperature) selects the next token from these logits. The key-value cache stores attention keys and values for all previous tokens so they do not need recomputation — without it, inference cost would scale as O(n²) in sequence length.

Quantisation converts float16/bfloat16 weights to lower-precision integers. NF4 (NormalFloat 4-bit) is designed specifically for neural network weight distributions, which are approximately Gaussian. Rather than uniform integer quantisation, NF4 allocates more quantisation levels near zero (where weights are dense) and fewer near the extremes (where weights are sparse). This produces lower quantisation error than int4 at the same bit width.

One of the most important advantages of open weights is full fine-tuning control. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is the standard efficient fine-tuning method: it freezes the base model weights and trains small rank-decomposition matrices added to selected layers. A 7B model has ~7B parameters; a LoRA adapter with rank r=16 adds only ~8M trainable parameters — a 1000× reduction in GPU memory required for training.

# pip install transformers peft accelerate datasets
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments, Trainer
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from datasets import Dataset
import torch


def fine_tune_with_lora(
    base_model_id: str = "mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3",
    train_data: list[dict] | None = None,
    output_dir: str = "./lora-finetuned",
):
    """
    Fine-tune an open-weight model with LoRA on custom instruction pairs.

    train_data format: [{"instruction": "...", "response": "..."}, ...]
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(base_model_id)
    tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        base_model_id,
        torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        device_map="auto",
    )

    # Configure LoRA: target attention projection layers
    lora_config = LoraConfig(
        task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
        r=16,            # rank — controls adapter capacity
        lora_alpha=32,   # scaling factor
        lora_dropout=0.05,
        target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],  # which layers to adapt
    )
    model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
    model.print_trainable_parameters()
    # Output: trainable params: 8,388,608 || all params: 7,250,436,096 || trainable%: 0.12%

    # Prepare dataset
    if train_data is None:
        train_data = [
            {"instruction": "Summarise this in one sentence.", "response": "This is a summary."},
        ]

    def format_example(ex):
        text = f"<s>[INST] {ex['instruction']} [/INST] {ex['response']} </s>"
        tokens = tokenizer(text, truncation=True, max_length=512, padding="max_length")
        tokens["labels"] = tokens["input_ids"].copy()
        return tokens

    dataset = Dataset.from_list(train_data).map(format_example, remove_columns=["instruction", "response"])

    training_args = TrainingArguments(
        output_dir=output_dir,
        num_train_epochs=3,
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        bf16=True,
        logging_steps=10,
        save_strategy="epoch",
    )

    trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=training_args, train_dataset=dataset)
    trainer.train()
    model.save_pretrained(output_dir)
    tokenizer.save_pretrained(output_dir)
    print(f"LoRA adapter saved to {output_dir}")
    return model

A law firm needs to answer questions about confidential contracts without any data leaving their premises. The solution: a local open-weight model serving a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline, running entirely on on-premise hardware.

# Full air-gapped RAG pipeline using open-weight model via Ollama
# Requirements: ollama running locally, model pulled with `ollama pull llama3`

import ollama
from typing import Iterator

# Simple in-memory document store (replace with vector DB in production)
class DocumentStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self.docs: list[tuple[str, str]] = []   # (source, text)
        self.embeddings = None

    def add(self, source: str, text: str):
        self.docs.append((source, text))

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
        """
        Simple keyword search — replace with embedding similarity in production.
        Ollama also supports embedding models (e.g. nomic-embed-text).
        """
        query_words = set(query.lower().split())
        scores = []
        for source, text in self.docs:
            overlap = len(query_words & set(text.lower().split()))
            scores.append((overlap, source, text))
        scores.sort(reverse=True)
        return [(s, t) for _, s, t in scores[:top_k]]


def answer_with_rag(store: DocumentStore, question: str, model: str = "llama3") -> str:
    """
    Retrieve relevant context, then generate an answer — fully local.
    """
    contexts = store.search(question, top_k=3)
    context_str = "\n\n---\n\n".join(
        f"[Source: {src}]\n{text}" for src, text in contexts
    )

    prompt = f"""You are a legal assistant. Answer the question using ONLY the provided context.
If the answer is not in the context, say "I cannot find this in the provided documents."

Context:
{context_str}

Question: {question}
Answer:"""

    response = ollama.chat(
        model=model,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        options={"temperature": 0.1},   # low temperature for factual tasks
    )
    return response["message"]["content"]


# Usage
store = DocumentStore()
store.add("contract_A.pdf", "The payment terms require net-30 settlement from invoice date. Late fees accrue at 1.5% per month.")
store.add("contract_B.pdf", "Confidentiality obligations survive for 5 years after contract termination.")

answer = answer_with_rag(store, "What are the late payment terms?")
print(answer)
# Output: The payment terms require net-30 settlement from invoice date.
#         Late fees accrue at 1.5% per month. [Source: contract_A.pdf]
Loading the base model instead of the instruction-tuned variant

# WRONG: Base model predicts next tokens — it will complete your prompt,
# not answer it. It was trained to continue text, not follow instructions.
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
model_id = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B"   # base model
# Prompting with "Q: What is 2+2? A:" may work, but is fragile and verbose.

# RIGHT: Use the instruction-tuned / chat variant
model_id = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"   # RLHF/DPO aligned
# Use the model's chat template for reliable instruction following
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}]
tokens = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(messages, return_tensors="pt", add_generation_prompt=True)
# The chat template wraps messages in the correct special tokens
Ignoring the licence before building a product

LLaMA 3's Community Licence restricts use for services with more than 700 million monthly active users and prohibits using outputs to train competing models. Qwen and Mistral's Apache 2.0 licence imposes no such restrictions. Always check licence compatibility with your product's use case and user scale before committing to a model family in production.
Pin to a specific model revision:
Use revision='abc1234' in from_pretrained() to lock to a specific commit. Hugging Face Hub models can be updated without notice; a pinned revision ensures reproducibility.
Safetensors format is safe against arbitrary code execution (unlike PyTorch .bin files which use pickle). Set use_safetensors=True in from_pretrained().
Install flash-attn and set attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' in from_pretrained() for 2–4× speedup on sequences > 512 tokens.
Use model.get_memory_footprint() and measure peak VRAM with nvidia-smi during a representative inference batch. Include KV-cache in your estimate (grows with batch_size × context_length × n_layers).
Implement request batching in your server:
Transformer inference is GPU-bound; serving requests one at a time leaves >80% of GPU compute idle. Use vLLM or TGI, which implement continuous batching and PagedAttention for high throughput.

For production serving, vLLM is the standard inference server. It implements PagedAttention — a memory allocator for KV-cache that eliminates fragmentation and enables batch sizes 10–30× larger than naive serving. With vLLM, a single A100 80GB can serve a Mistral 7B model at ~1,000 tokens/second across concurrent requests.

# Install and start vLLM server (OpenAI-compatible API)
pip install vllm

python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 \
    --dtype bfloat16 \
    --max-model-len 4096 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
    --port 8000

# Query with standard OpenAI client
# from openai import OpenAI
# client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="unused")
Full control; no data leaves infra
Data sent to vendor; DPA required
Fixed infra cost; zero marginal per token
70B models near GPT-3.5; smaller models behind frontier
Terms of service; no weights access

Open-weight models introduce security risks that closed API usage does not. The weights file is executable code in the sense that loading a malicious .bin (pickle) file can execute arbitrary Python. Always use safetensors format and download only from verified sources (official model cards, or checksum-verified mirrors). The safetensors format explicitly forbids executable code, limiting the attack surface to crafted weight values — a much harder attack.

Alignment removal is a realistic threat. Open weights can be fine-tuned to remove safety guardrails. If your deployment relies on model-level content filtering, you must implement additional filtering at the application layer (input/output classifiers, keyword filters, human review for high-stakes outputs). Do not treat RLHF-aligned open-weight models as equivalent in safety to commercially operated APIs, which enforce server-side content policies independently of the model weights.
provide publicly downloadable checkpoints, enabling fully local inference with no API dependency, no per-token cost, and no data sent to third parties.
Leading families (LLaMA 3, Mistral, Gemma 2, Qwen 2.5, Phi-3) span 1B–405B parameters and cover most practical use cases from edge deployment to multi-GPU cluster inference.
Quantisation (4-bit NF4, 8-bit int) reduces VRAM by 2–4×, enabling a 7B model to run on a consumer GPU; quality degradation is small for most tasks.
adapts open-weight models to custom domains with ~8M trainable parameters and a single GPU, compared to billions of parameters in full fine-tuning.
Choose open-weight when data privacy requirements, high token volume, or fine-tuning needs make APIs impractical; choose closed APIs for frontier capability, zero ops overhead, or low volume.
Licence compatibility is a first-class concern: Apache 2.0 (Mistral, Qwen) is most permissive; LLaMA 3 Community imposes scale and redistribution restrictions.
Use vLLM for production serving (continuous batching, PagedAttention) and safetensors format for loading (prevents pickle-based code execution attacks).
